Title: RNAi of the sesquiterpene cyclase gene for phytoalexin production impairs pre- and post-invasive resistance to potato blight pathogens

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key enzyme for production of lubimin and rishitin
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Vetispiradiene precursor(s) leading to lubimin and rishitin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes farnesyl diphosphate to sesquiterpene hydrocarbon precursor(s) in the pathway leading to the potato phytoalexins lubimin and rishitin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies immediate precursor pool for target phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial defense against potato blight pathogens; contributes to resistance to Phytophthora infestans and Alternaria solani
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RNAi silencing abolished lubimin and rishitin accumulation in infected tubers and increased susceptibility to P. infestans and A. solani; in leaves it increased susceptibility despite undetectable lubimin/rishitin, implying PVS-mediated defensive sesquiterpenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi silencing in transgenic potato; RT-qPCR of PVS1-PVS4; siRNA detection; PVS3 promoter:GUS pathogen induction assay; TLC/HPLC metabolite profiling; antifungal assays; pathogen biomass qPCR; infection microscopy
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in tubers and leaves by hyphal wall components and by avirulent or virulent P. infestans; PVS3 expression is much higher than PVS1/2/4 in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced around infection sites in tubers and leaves after P. infestans inoculation; PVS3 promoter active in neighboring cells around dead/colonized cells
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S constitutive promoter for RNAi; native PVS3 promoter for GUS reporter
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Inoculation with avirulent or virulent Phytophthora infestans; inoculation with Alternaria solani; treatment with P. infestans hyphal wall components
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious developmental defects in tubers and leaves were reported in RNAi lines under normal growth
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RNAi targeting a conserved PVS region strongly suppressed PVS1-PVS4 transcripts in potato tubers and leaves. 2. PVS silencing eliminated accumulation of the sesquiterpenoid phytoalexins lubimin and rishitin in infected tubers and abolished associated antifungal activity. 3. PVS-silenced tubers and leaves showed impaired resistance to avirulent and/or virulent P. infestans, with increased pathogen growth in initially infected cells. 4. PVS-silenced leaves were also more susceptible to A. solani, indicating a role in pre-invasive defense likely via unidentified sesquiterpenoid compounds. 5. PVS3 showed strong pathogen-inducible expression and appears to be a central family member in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintain or enhance PVS activity to strengthen potato disease resistance linked to sesquiterpenoid phytoalexin metabolism; stacking with upstream pathway genes such as HMGR may improve efficacy
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The focal experimental target is the PVS gene family, especially PVS3 used for RNAi trigger and promoter analysis. In leaves, lubimin and rishitin were not detected, so PVS likely supports other unidentified defensive sesquiterpenoids or volatile compounds. The paper does not resolve individual catalytic differences among PVS1-PVS4.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Mevalonate/isoprenoid pathway feeding sesquiterpenoid phytoalexin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Described as another key gene/enzyme for phytoalexin synthesis; differential regulation may explain stronger incompatible-interaction phytoalexin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate to supply isoprenoid precursors for sesquiterpenoid phytoalexin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for target phytoalexins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: HMGR2 was induced after avirulent P. infestans infection and is proposed to contribute to high phytoalexin accumulation, but it was not directly manipulated in this study
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression analysis / cited prior enzyme activity evidence
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in leaves after avirulent P. infestans inoculation; HMGR activity rises more rapidly in incompatible than compatible tuber interactions according to cited work
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Avirulent versus virulent P. infestans infection
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HMGR2 expression was induced in potato leaves after avirulent P. infestans infection. 2. The paper treats HMGR2 as another key gene for phytoalexin synthesis. 3. Differential HMGR regulation is suggested to underlie stronger phytoalexin accumulation in incompatible interactions.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential companion target with PVS to increase sesquiterpenoid phytoalexin production, though direct functional validation was not performed here
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HMGR2 is discussed and expression-validated, but it is not a primary experimental focal gene compared with PVS; included as a secondary core pathway gene only because the Results explicitly characterize its inducible expression as another key gene for phytoalexin synthesis.
